definitions.example: For example, Newton's first law of motion states that an object at rest will remain at rest, and an object in motion will continue to move with a constant velocity, unless acted upon by an external force.
definitions.definition: A law of motion is a statement that describes the relationship between the motion of an object and the forces acting upon it. There are three laws of motion, which were formulated by Sir Isaac Newton. These laws provide a fundamental understanding of how objects move and respond to forces.

definitions.example: For example, a moving car will continue to move in a straight line unless the brakes are applied, which is an external force that changes its motion.
definitions.definition: Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist changes in its motion. According to Newton's first law of motion, an object will maintain its state of motion unless an external force acts upon it.

definitions.example: For example, a person pushing a wall is applying a contact force, while the gravitational force acting on an object is a non-contact force.
definitions.definition: A force is a push or a pull that causes an object to change its motion. Forces can be classified into contact forces and non-contact forces.

definitions.example: For example, a heavier object has a greater mass and will be more difficult to accelerate than a lighter object.
definitions.definition: Mass is a measure of the amount of matter in an object. The mass of an object is a measure of its resistance to changes in its motion.

definitions.example: For example, the weight of a person on Earth is equal to their mass multiplied by the acceleration due to gravity on Earth.
definitions.definition: Weight is the force exerted on an object due to gravity. The weight of an object is equal to its mass multiplied by the acceleration due to gravity.

definitions.example: For example, a moving car has a large amount of momentum, which is difficult to change without applying a significant force.
definitions.definition: Momentum is the product of an object's mass and velocity. Momentum is a measure of an object's tendency to keep moving in a straight line.

definitions.example: For example, hitting a ball with a bat applies an impulse to the ball, which changes its motion.
definitions.definition: Impulse is the product of a force and the time over which it is applied. Impulse is a measure of the change in momentum of an object.

definitions.example: For example, the friction between the tires of a car and the road causes the car to slow down when the brakes are applied.
definitions.definition: Friction is a force that opposes motion between two surfaces that are in contact. There are different types of friction, including static friction and kinetic friction.

definitions.example: For example, a moving car is an example of translational motion, while the spinning of a top is an example of rotational motion.
definitions.definition: Motion is a change in the position of an object with respect to time. Motion can be classified into different types, including translational motion and rotational motion.

definitions.example: For example, a car traveling at 60 km/h in a northerly direction has a velocity of 60 km/h north.
definitions.definition: Velocity is a measure of an object's speed in a particular direction. Velocity is a vector quantity and has both magnitude and direction.

definitions.example: For example, a car accelerating from 0 to 60 km/h in 10 seconds has an acceleration of 6 m/s^2.
definitions.definition: Acceleration is a measure of the rate of change of an object's velocity. Acceleration is a vector quantity and has both magnitude and direction.

definitions.example: For example, a moving car will continue to move in a straight line unless the brakes are applied, which is an external force that changes its motion.
definitions.definition: Newton's first law of motion states that an object at rest will remain at rest, and an object in motion will continue to move with a constant velocity, unless acted upon by an external force.

definitions.example: For example, a heavier car will have a smaller acceleration than a lighter car when the same force is applied.
definitions.definition: Newton's second law of motion states that the acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the force applied and inversely proportional to its mass.

definitions.example: For example, when a person pushes a wall, the wall pushes the person back with an equal and opposite force.
definitions.definition: Newton's third law of motion states that for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.

definitions.example: For example, in a perfectly elastic collision, the total momentum of the system remains constant.
definitions.definition: Conservation of momentum states that the total momentum of a closed system remains constant over time. This means that the momentum before a collision is equal to the momentum after the collision.

definitions.example: For example, lifting a heavy box to a higher position requires work to be done against gravity.
definitions.definition: Work is the product of a force and the displacement of an object in the direction of the force.

definitions.example: For example, a moving car has kinetic energy, while a ball at the top of a hill has potential energy.
definitions.definition: Energy is the ability to do work. There are different types of energy, including kinetic energy and potential energy.

definitions.example: For example, turning a screw requires a torque to be applied to the screw head.
definitions.definition: Torque is a measure of the twisting force that causes an object to rotate. Torque is a vector quantity and has both magnitude and direction.

definitions.example: For example, a spinning top has a large amount of angular momentum, which makes it difficult to stop.
definitions.definition: Angular momentum is the product of an object's mass, velocity, and distance from the axis of rotation. Angular momentum is a measure of an object's tendency to keep rotating.
